titleStmt.title: Letter from William Tyrrell Jr. to Robert Tyrrell, 24 December 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Tyrrell Jr.

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
The letters of William Upton Tyrrell ('Willie') to his father William J.H. Tyrrell, mother Elizabeth, sister Minnie and brother Robert are a notable example of wartime correspondence. The letters - nearly 200 in total - are mostly written by William to his family while in active service with the Royal Irish Rifles in France. Willie discusses his life and the lives of other soldiers in the trenches, as well as life at Ballindoolin, the family estate.
The family name 'Tyrrell' is sometimes misspelled 'Tyrell' in correspondence to the family.
In the present letter, Willie expresses gratitude for his Christmas parcels. He purports that he had the most of all the men.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
24/12/17 My dear Bobby Thank you very much indeed
for the splendid photograph. It is
very good indeed of you and
is exactly what I wanted. You
were great to think of such a thing. Well this is Xmas eve and I am
sitting before a huge fire eating toffee
which came in one of those magnificent
parvels. I got three large ones last
night late. One containing apples, one
some delicious SOS cakes, and the
other delicious odds and ends. Invited
Moore to tea to have some cakes and
he thought they were delicious too. Really the amount of parcels you
all sent is enormous and you
have no idea what a treat they are,
because we are not near any places
where we can buy things and of
course everything is scarce in
such weather in the country. It is
wonderful how very quickly all the
mails came. The apples were in
perfect condition and really a
great treat. Every one has been
getting parcels but no one got
half as many as I did and none
of them nearly so nice. I have
just sampled the big cake and
it is just like everything else.
absolutely lof hole. I wish I had
Garrett here to eat a chunk of it. I hope you are getting some
shooting though if your weather
is like ours it will be bad
for it. There is a thaw on
tonight but I think it is only
a temporary one. I have to keep shifting away
from the fire as I am getting
roasted. I got a good letter last night
too from Minnie which she
said would not arrive until
long after Xmas. I cannot think of anything
else to say so I will shut up
with very best love to all Your affie brother Willie
